# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Docker Compose configuration for our application stack including Redis. Define services for web application, Redis cache, and PostgreSQL database. Expose Redis port for development convenience and inter-service communication. This is for our containerized development environment.
# Category: redis_exposed_port

version: '3'  # Specify the version of docker compose file format to use (default value as specified in current Docker Compose specification)
services:   # Define services here, each service will be a separate container instance with its own configuration.
    webapp:     # Name for your application/service is defined by this name – it can contain lowercase letters and numbers but cannot start or end with an underscore (e.g., not allowed to use 'web_application') so we'll call our service "webapp" here as per requirement
      image: python-image   # Docker Image for the application/service is defined by this name – it can contain lowercase letters and numbers but cannot start or end with an underscore (e.g., not allowed to use 'python_application') so we'll call our service "webapp" here as per requirement
      ports:   # Define how the container should expose its services on a network – this is done by mapping host machine port(s) onto containers internal networks using either named volumes or bind mounts. Here, both Redis and PostgreSQL are exposed to allow communication between different parts of our application stack (webapp -> redis & db).
        - "5001:5001"   # This maps the host machine port 5001 onto container's internal network on port 5001. The format is <host-machine-port>:<container-internal-network-port> – multiple ports can be mapped in a comma separated list, e.g., "8080:9292", where the host machine and container's internal network are exposed on different port numbers
      environment:   # Define any environmental variables for your service – these will override those set by Docker itself (e.g.: DEBUG=True) in a .env file that is included with each application’s source code, or as command line arguments when starting the container using docker run -e). Here we'll define environment variable "DEBUG" to be True for our webapp service
        - DEBUG=true   # This sets an environmental variables 'debugging mode'. The value of this is set by Docker itself and can either remain unset (default), or override it with a specific setting. In the case above, we've defined that debug should always be true for our webapp service
      volumes:  # Define any data to persist across container restarts e.g., database files will not survive between different docker-compose up commands so they are persisted in /var/lib/docker and can therefore also serve as a backup if the host machine goes down or needs reinstating after being shutted off
        - ./data:/app   # This binds an existing directory on your local filesystem to one inside our container at '/app' – this allows data persistence. The source of truth for where Docker stores its files is /var/lib/docker, but we can also use it as a backup if the host machine goes down or needs reinstating after being shutted off
      depends_on:   # Define what services your service should depend on – this means that when one of those other dependencies finishes running before yours. Here our webapp will only start once postgres is up and we've defined it as a dependency for the 'webapp'. This ensures all three are started at same time
        - db   # The name or ID (depends_on) to wait until this service has been created, then proceed with starting. In our case that would be when database server is up and running before we start web app as well – the 'db' in depends-on means it will block till postgres starts
      links:   # Define any services your application should link against e.g., for communication between different parts of a service, or to use an external redis instance (not included here) that is not part of this compose file and can be accessed via the hostname in our webapp container using docker-compose run --rm webapp redis_service command
        - db   # This links 'db' with itself. In other words, we are saying to Docker Compose when it needs an instance (or service) of a database that is not defined within this compose file but can be accessed via the hostname in our current webapp container using docker-compose run --rm webapp redis_service command
      restart:   # Define how your application should behave if one or more containers are stopped – here we'll define it to always start up again after a failure. This is useful for development and testing, but not recommended in production environments as the risk of data loss can be high (unless you have backups).
        - unless-stopped   # If this service fails then Docker Compose will keep running until manually stopped by an external process or system event such as a host machine reboot. In our case that would mean if 'db' is not up and started, webapp won’t restart it automatically but instead the application might stop working due to some issues with db
      networks:   # Define how your services should connect together on network – here we define one named "backend" for all of our other service. This allows us to have a single IP address and port that can be used by any container in this compose file, regardless if they are running inside or outside the same Docker host
        - backend   # Name is defined as 'backend' here – it will allow containers within network named "backend" (and all other networks) to communicate with each others. This can be used for inter-service communication and also networking in a multi container environment like Kubernetes or Docker Swarm
